person.prominent_name_reason: Wife of Gov. Caleb Strong — 6th and 10th Governor of Massachusetts (1800-1807, 1812-1816), U.S. Senator, Founding Father, delegate to the Philadelphia Convention of 1787. Daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton (H00264). 5th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker.
person.gravestone_inscription: In Memory of Mrs. Sarah Strong, the wife of Caleb Strong and daughter of the Rev John Hooker who having early devoted herself to the service of her Redeemer and through life exemplified the virtues & graces of his religion gently breathed out her spirit in humble reliance upon his power and love on the 12th day of February AD 1817 in the 60th year of her age.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of smallpox before her wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev. John Hooker pastor of Northampton MA died of smallpox in February 1777 when she was 19. Caleb Strong, a lawyer who had joined her father's congregation five years earlier, married her in November 1777 — nine months after the funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Matriarch of the Strong-Hooker-Dwight branch
narrative_blocks.body: Mother of nine children with Gov. Caleb Strong including Clarissa Strong Dwight, who married Timothy Dwight Jr. in 1809 and produced the New Haven Dwight-Lyman cluster — three sons of whom were Yale-educated and one of whom Sam's tree records as ancestor of two generations of Yale presidents.

H00525.prominent_name_reason: Wife of Gov. Caleb Strong — 6th and 10th Governor of Massachusetts (1800-1807, 1812-1816), U.S. Senator, Founding Father, delegate to the Philadelphia Convention of 1787. Daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton (H00264). 5th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker.
H00525.gravestone_inscription: In Memory of Mrs. Sarah Strong, the wife of Caleb Strong and daughter of the Rev John Hooker who having early devoted herself to the service of her Redeemer and through life exemplified the virtues & graces of his religion gently breathed out her spirit in humble reliance upon his power and love on the 12th day of February AD 1817 in the 60th year of her age.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of smallpox before her wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev. John Hooker pastor of Northampton MA died of smallpox in February 1777 when she was 19. Caleb Strong, a lawyer who had joined her father's congregation five years earlier, married her in November 1777 — nine months after the funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Matriarch of the Strong-Hooker-Dwight branch
narrative_blocks.body: Mother of nine children with Gov. Caleb Strong including Clarissa Strong Dwight, who married Timothy Dwight Jr. in 1809 and produced the New Haven Dwight-Lyman cluster — three sons of whom were Yale-educated and one of whom Sam's tree records as ancestor of two generations of Yale presidents.

political_offices.title: U.S. Senator (Class 2), Massachusetts
political_offices.notes: Member of the inaugural U.S. Senate

political_offices.title: Massachusetts Constitutional Convention
political_offices.notes: Member of committee that drafted the Massachusetts State Constitution

X00117.prominent_name_reason: Gov. Caleb Strong — Founding Father; delegate to 1787 Philadelphia Convention; helped draft Massachusetts State Constitution (1779); U.S. Senator in inaugural Senate; 6th and 10th Governor of Massachusetts; instrumental in drafting Judiciary Act of 1789 and 11th Amendment; worked on Quock Walker cases establishing slavery incompatible with Massachusetts constitution. Married Sarah Hooker (H00525), daughter of Rev. John Hooker of Northampton.

notable.notable_blurb: Governor of Massachusetts 1800-1807 (Federalist) and U.S. Senator

education.notes: Temporarily blinded by smallpox shortly after graduation — prevented him from military service during the Revolution

narrative_blocks.header: Married his pastor's daughter, 1777
narrative_blocks.body: He joined Rev. John Hooker's Northampton congregation in 1772 at age 27. Five years later, when Rev. Hooker died of smallpox in February 1777, the newly-licensed lawyer married his late pastor's daughter Sarah Hooker in November 1777. The Hooker name thus passed directly into the family of a future Founding Father.

narrative_blocks.header: U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, 1789-1796
narrative_blocks.body: In 1788 he was elected as one of the first two U.S. Senators from Massachusetts and served 1789-1796 in the Federalist majority of the early Senate. He had previously served at the 1787 Constitutional Convention as a Massachusetts delegate, though he left Philadelphia before the document was signed because of family illness.

narrative_blocks.header: Federalist Governor of Massachusetts, 1800-1807
narrative_blocks.body: He served seven consecutive one-year terms as Federalist Governor of Massachusetts 1800-1807, then a four-term return 1812-1816. During the War of 1812 his Federalist stance against the war and against New England troop mobilization drew Madison administration ire but was popular in Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of nine, source of the Strong-Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: With his wife Sarah Hooker Strong he had nine children: 5 sons and 4 daughters including Clarissa Strong Dwight, the matriarch of the New Haven Dwight-Lyman branch. The family thereby connected the Massachusetts political elite to the Hooker founding line of Hartford CT.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College class of 1797. Subsequent career as lawyer at Coal Grove, Ohio.

narrative_blocks.header: First of the Strong-Hooker line west
narrative_blocks.body: When Theodore Strong moved his family from Northampton to Coal Grove, Ohio, he carried the Hooker-Strong inheritance west of the Appalachians for the first time. His father Governor Caleb Strong had governed Massachusetts from Northampton; Theodore died at the Ohio frontier in 1855.

narrative_blocks.header: His obituary named him the Governor's grandson
narrative_blocks.body: When Jeremiah Allen Strong died December 17, 1870 at Coal Grove, his obituary noted he was a son of Theodore Strong and a grandson of Caleb Strong of Massachusetts — the famous Federalist Governor. Three generations separated Coal Grove's coal fields from the Governor's Northampton mansion.

cross_connections.display_label: Husband Thompson's deputation visited Capron's Madura mission

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband outlived her by forty-four years
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Elizabeth Strong Thompson died May 11, 1857 at forty-eight years old in Somerville, Massachusetts — forty-four years before her husband Rev. Augustus Thompson, who lived to eighty-nine and served as senior pastor of Eliot Congregational Church in Roxbury for fifty-nine unbroken years. She saw five children born; he outlived three of them.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 1811; died at twenty years old
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Ann Strong was born June 3, 1811 in Northampton and died August 17, 1831 at twenty years old — the third child of Theodore and Martha Allen Strong. No marriage is recorded in the Hooker genealogy.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Sarah Hooker; died young in Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Strong carried the exact name of her grandmother — born September 13, 1817 at Northampton, she moved west to Ohio and died August 26, 1840 at twenty-two. She was the third generation to carry the Hooker name forward.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Sarah Hooker after grandmother Sarah Hooker Strong (H00525), continuing the name into the third generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at five years old in Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Augusta Strong was born March 30, 1822 and died November 10, 1827 at five years old — the youngest of Theodore Strong's five children, and the last to die young in a family that saw three children die before thirty.

narrative_blocks.header: Fell into boiling water at age two, 1783
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong was born February 19, 1781 and died October 26, 1783 at two and a half — the Hooker genealogy records she was 'scalded by falling into a tub of boiling water.' She was the second child of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong.

HD0371.prominent_name_reason: Through her marriage to Timothy Dwight Jr., Clarissa Strong connected Thomas Hooker's line to the Edwards-Dwight family. Her father-in-law Rev. Timothy Dwight Sr. was 8th President of Yale (1795-1817); his mother Mary Edwards was daughter of Jonathan Edwards; Jonathan Edwards's wife Sarah Pierpont descended from Thomas Hooker through Mary Hooker Pierpont → James Pierpont → Sarah Pierpont Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Massachusetts governor's daughter married a hardware merchant
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1809 at age 25 she married Timothy Dwight Jr. — hardware merchant, son of 8th Yale President 'Pope' Dwight — at her father's Northampton home. Her father had just completed seven Federalist terms as MA Governor; the marriage tied the Strong political dynasty to the Dwight intellectual one.

narrative_blocks.header: Double Hooker descent for all seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Timothy Dwight Jr. was himself a Hooker descendant through his mother Mary Edwards Dwight, daughter of Esther Edwards. With her own Hooker descent through her mother Sarah Hooker, all seven of their children carried two independent lines of Thomas Hooker ancestry.

narrative_blocks.header: Her family’s papers fill a Yale archive
narrative_blocks.body: The Dwight family papers at Yale preserve the correspondence, sermons, and photographs of the clan she married into — Edwards, Hooker, Lyman, Strong, Woodbridge, and Woolsey relations all represented. She rests at Grove Street, steps from the college her father-in-law led.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

career.notes: 'A man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous.' Donated $5,000 endowment for Yale's Dwight Professorship for Didactic Theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Endowed Yale's Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology
narrative_blocks.body: A New Haven hardware merchant remembered as 'a man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals.' He donated $5,000 to Yale to endow the Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology — named for his father Pope Dwight rather than himself.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Strong-Hooker line, fathered Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: In 1809 he married Clarissa Strong — daughter of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong, herself a Hooker descendant — bringing two independent Hooker descent lines into his children. Their son Timothy Dwight V became the 12th President of Yale 1886-1899.

narrative_blocks.header: Hardware merchant of liberal culture and fervid piety
narrative_blocks.body: Per the family memoir he was 'a man of liberal culture, of fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous; he believed in justice and right, and hated false shows and shams.' He had a 'fine intellectual face' and was 'of large frame and vigorous health' — a portrait of the merchant-evangelical type of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her dead sister's husband, raised the sons
narrative_blocks.body: She married Dr. Jonathan Huntington Lyman Jr. in New Haven on 2 January 1855 — fourteen months after her sister Julia's tuberculosis death — and raised Julia's two surviving sons as her own. She bore no biological children and remained at Hadley MA for the marriage's 35 years until her 1886 death at 71, four years before Lyman's own.

cross_connections.notes: Both gen 7. HD1982 also doubly Hooker-descended through mother Clarissa Strong (HD0371). The Dwight family genealogy (written by HD1990's brother Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight) describes Sophia as a lady of "great personal beauty and sweetness of character."
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.

cross_connections.notes: Both gen 7. HD1982 also doubly Hooker-descended through mother Clarissa Strong (HD0371). The Dwight family genealogy (written by HD1990's brother Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight) describes Sophia as a lady of "great personal beauty and sweetness of character."
cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both grandchildren of Yale president Timothy Dwight.
A Strong cousin named his son Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with wife Sophia Dwight Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: His bride was also his cousin, Sophia Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: John William Dwight married Sophia Dwight in June 1845 — his first cousin, daughter of Dr. Benjamin Woolsey and Sophia (Strong) Dwight of Clinton, New York. Both were grandchildren of President Timothy Dwight of Yale; cousin marriage was legal and common in New England.

narrative_blocks.header: His saltpeter business fed the gunpowder trade
narrative_blocks.body: John William Dwight established himself in New Haven as a manufacturer of saltpetre — potassium nitrate, the primary oxidizer in gunpowder. He operated through the 1840s and into the Civil War decade, when demand for gunpowder ingredients reached its wartime peak.

career.location: New Haven, New Haven County, Connecticut
career.notes: Manufacturer of saltpetre (potassium nitrate) at New Haven CT. Per EH 1909 #2681.

cross_connections.display_label: Father-in-law: Union chaplain who served Bull Run to Appomattox

institutions.notes: Andover Theological Seminary after Yale 1838; ordained Congregational minister; settled at Gorham ME parish.

burial.plot_notes: Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover MA — with 2nd wife Elizabeth McGregor Adams Dwight + 2 of their daughters

narrative_blocks.header: Yale theologian and Gorham Maine pastor
narrative_blocks.body: Born 30 April 1820 New Haven CT to the hardware merchant Timothy Dwight Jr and his MA-Governor's-daughter wife Clarissa Strong. Yale BA 1838 followed by Andover Theological Seminary; ordained Congregational minister; settled at Gorham ME — where he met his first wife and later his second.

narrative_blocks.header: Third sister-replacement marriage isn't this one
narrative_blocks.body: His own pattern was DIFFERENT from the Lyman + Anthony sister-replacement marriages in his family. His first wife Lucy Waterman died at 38 in 1861; three years later in 1864 he married not her sister but his parish's Sunday-school superintendent's daughter — Elizabeth McGregor Adams, child of his Gorham predecessor Rev. John Ripley Adams.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into a Civil War chaplain's family, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1864 — while his future father-in-law was serving as chaplain of the 121st New York Volunteer Infantry in the Army of the Potomac — he married Elizabeth McGregor Adams at her Gorham ME family home at age 44. Her father Rev. John Ripley Adams would survive Appomattox by one year then die of brain inflammation caused by war stress.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived both wives and one son
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Waterman 1861 at 38; Elizabeth Adams 1879 Independence Day at 42; son Edward Huntington 1893 at 37 — three premature deaths in his immediate family before his own 1890 death at Hadley MA at age 70. His two younger daughters by the second marriage lived to 88 and 90.

career.notes: Settled in Gorham ME after Yale + Andover; met both wives there.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in Jamaica, leaving three children under six
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Strong Dwight died December 4, 1853 in Jamaica, West Indies at twenty-nine — her youngest child Francis had just been born in Chicago months before, and the oldest was only five years old. Within thirteen months of her death, her widower Dr. Jonathan Huntington Lyman married her sister Mary Woolsey Dwight, keeping the children within the Dwight family.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven of Timothy Jr's, died at thirty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1825 New Haven to Timothy Dwight Jr and Clarissa Strong, she was one of seven siblings carrying double Hooker descent. She died in 1864 at age 39 — the same year her cousin Mary Dwight Anthony died in Chicago, also age 39 — compounding family losses in the war year.

narrative_blocks.header: Last of Timothy Jr's children, died at thirty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1831 to Timothy Dwight Jr and Clarissa Strong as their seventh child, she died in 1870 at age 39. With sister Julia (TB 1853 age 29), brother John William's wife Sophia Dwight (1863 age 45), and sister Mary (1864 age 39), she completed a generation of premature deaths in the Timothy Jr branch.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved to Chicago; made paper bags
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Dwight moved from New Haven to Chicago and built a paper bag manufacturing business — an industry rapidly growing mid-century. His son Timothy worked alongside him as a civil engineer.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the Governor; died at two
narrative_blocks.body: Caleb Strong Dwight was born September 1812, named for his grandfather Governor Caleb Strong of Massachusetts. He died July 7, 1814 at about two years old.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died 1816; first named Edward Strong
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Strong Dwight was born in 1816 and died the same year, named for his uncle Edward Strong who had died at twenty-two after Harvard. His parents later gave the same name to a second son.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Sarah Hooker; died at fifteen years old
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Dwight was born July 20, 1822 in New Haven, carrying the name of her great-grandmother Sarah Hooker Strong — the third generation of women in this family to be named Sarah Hooker. She died May 8, 1838 at fifteen years old.

naming_inspiration.description: Named Sarah Hooker after great-grandmother H00525 — third generation to carry this name alongside H02675 and H04225.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her mother; died in infancy
narrative_blocks.body: Clarissa Dwight was born September 22, 1826 and died January 1828 at fifteen months, named for her mother Clarissa Strong Dwight. Of their nine children, four died before adulthood.

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard College class of 1803. Practiced law in Northampton Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Northampton; never left; buried there
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Strong was born in Northampton in 1785, graduated Harvard in 1803, and returned to practice law — never leaving the town where his father the Governor had built his career. He died there October 25, 1863.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his ten children died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis and Mariah Chester Strong raised ten children in Northampton, and three died in childhood: Elizabeth Chester at two, Julia Mitchell at two, Maria Chester at eight. A fourth son Theodore died at twenty-two the same month he graduated Williams College.

parents.father_research_notes: Hon. Stephen Chester, Sheriff of Hartford County CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at two years old in Northampton
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Chester Strong was born July 3, 1812 and died June 1, 1815 at about two years old. She was the first of Lewis Strong's ten children — and the first of three to die in childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician; lived to eighty-eight years old
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Strong became a physician — the only M.D. among Lewis and Mariah Chester Strong's ten Northampton children. Born April 6, 1814, he died in 1902 at approximately eighty-eight years old, outliving most of his siblings and his parents by decades.

marriages.notes: Daughter of Stephen Mix and Sophia (Coit) Mitchell of Wethersfield CT. d. Sept 4, 1843.
Daughter of Amos Morris and Mary (Lyman) Collins of Hartford CT.

narrative_blocks.header: He and wife Catherine both died in Montreal
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Caleb Strong married Catherine S. Mitchell on May 26, 1840, and she died in Montreal on September 4, 1843 — buried at Cimetiere Mont-Royal. Four years later, on January 4, 1847, he died in the same city at thirty, having remarried Maria Elizabeth Collins just eight months before his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the sister who died at two
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Chester Strong (born September 1, 1817) carried the name of her older sister who had died in 1815 at two years old. She married Yale 1833 graduate William Matson and died January 29, 1867.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at two; third child lost young
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Mitchell Strong was born January 16, 1820 and died November 20, 1822 at two years old. She was the third of Lewis Strong's ten children to die in childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Married 1848; dead in New York at twenty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Lewis Mitchell Strong married Mary Frances Carpenter in 1848 and died in New York City on October 5, 1850 at twenty-eight. He left his wife a widow two years into their marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived his wife by eighteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Myra Peters Brown Strong died May 5, 1891 in Natick, Massachusetts at sixty-five. Stephen Chester Strong survived her by eighteen years, dying March 26, 1909 in New London County, Connecticut at eighty-five — the longest-lived of Lewis and Mariah Chester Strong's ten children.

education.institution_blurb: Williams College class of 1848; died the same month.

institutions.institution_blurb: Williams College class of 1848. Died August 31, 1848 — same month as graduation.

narrative_blocks.header: Graduated Williams College; died the same month
narrative_blocks.body: Theodore Strong graduated from Williams College in 1848 and died August 31, 1848 — the same month as his graduation, at twenty-two. He was the fourth of Lewis Strong's children to die young.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eight years old in Northampton
narrative_blocks.body: Maria Chester Strong was born April 18, 1828 in Northampton and died August 28, 1836 at eight years old — the fourth of Lewis Strong's children to die young. She was named Maria Chester after her mother Mariah Chester Strong.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband outlived her by twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Anne Strong married Jonathan Newton Harris — New London mayor, civic philanthropist, and one of Connecticut's leading merchants — on July 19, 1869, as his second wife. She died December 19, 1916 in New London at eighty-four; Harris had preceded her by twenty years, dying in 1896.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rev. Phoenix in 1822 — his third wife
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong married Rev. Alexander Phoenix on October 28, 1822 at thirty-five — and she was his third wife, a detail EH records without comment. They lived successively at Chicopee, Massachusetts, New Haven, Connecticut, and Harlem, New York, before she died at Paterson, New Jersey in 1856.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Sarah after the sister who died
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Strong (born July 22, 1787) was named after her older sister Sarah who had died by scalding in a tub of boiling water at age two in 1783. She died June 10, 1856 at Paterson, New Jersey, outliving her son Daniel Sidney Phoenix — a Yale student who died at sixteen in 1841 — by fifteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Clarissa Dwight; died at thirty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Clarissa Dwight Phoenix was born August 1, 1823 at Chicopee, named for her aunt Clarissa Strong Dwight. She died April 20, 1857 at thirty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale student; died at sixteen in New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Sidney Phoenix was born June 18, 1825 at Chicopee, Massachusetts, and died October 9, 1841 in New Haven, Connecticut at sixteen years old — while a student at Yale College. His mother Sarah died in 1856, having outlived this son by fifteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for an uncle; died at five
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Strong Phoenix was born June 2, 1827 at Chicopee, named for his uncle Edward Strong who had died at twenty-two. He died June 11, 1832 at five years old.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College class of 1850. Farmer at Vineland, New Jersey.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1850; farmer at Vineland, New Jersey
narrative_blocks.body: Sidney Phoenix graduated from Yale College in 1850 and married Julia Frances Metcalf on November 20, 1861. He settled as a farmer at Vineland, New Jersey — the only one of his three brothers to survive childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard graduate 1810; died at twenty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Strong graduated from Harvard College in 1810, following his father Caleb Strong who had graduated Harvard in 1764 — the same institution across two generations. He died May 6, 1813 at about twenty-two, just three years after graduation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-five; mother died the year before
narrative_blocks.body: Julia A. Strong was born in 1793 and died October 1, 1818 at about twenty-five. Her mother Sarah Hooker Strong had died February 12, 1817 — Julia outlived her mother by just eighteen months.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at four in Northampton, 1799
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe Strong was born March 22, 1795 and died September 10, 1799 at four years old. She was the eighth child of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong; both parents outlived her by more than fifteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in infancy; the last of nine children
narrative_blocks.body: Philip Strong was born February 27, 1799 and died August 17, 1800 — barely six months, the ninth and final child of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong. Of the nine children, four died before adulthood.

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.
His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably
